questions.question_text: Four friends throw the same biased coin. Heads/tails results: Ben 34/8, Helen 66/12, Paul 80/40, Sharif 120/40. Whose results give the best estimate of the probability of heads?
questions.explanation: More trials give a more reliable relative frequency; Sharif threw 160 times, the most of anyone.

answers.text: Paul's, because his sample is the most balanced
Ben's, because he has the highest proportion of heads
Sharif's, because he threw the coin the most times
Helen's, because her results are in simplest ratio

questions.question_text: Combining all the results in the table gives 300 heads from 400 throws. If the coin is thrown twice more, what is the best estimate of the probability it lands heads both times?
The weights of 40 babies: 2<w≤3 kg: 12 babies; 3<w≤4: 16; 4<w≤5: 9; 5<w≤6: 2; 6<w≤7: 1. What is the modal class?
questions.explanation: The class with the highest frequency (16 babies) is 3 < w ≤ 4.

questions.question_text: Using the same table of 40 babies (midpoints 2.5, 3.5, 4.5, 5.5, 6.5 kg with frequencies 12, 16, 9, 2, 1), estimate the mean weight.
One of the 40 babies is chosen at random. What is the probability that it weighs more than 5 kg?
questions.explanation: 2 + 1 = 3 babies weigh more than 5 kg, so the probability is 3/40.

questions.question_text: Allen finds the mode, median and range of 110, 114, 114, 115, 116, 117, 118. He then adds the value 90 to the data set. Which statistics change?
questions.explanation: The mode stays 114; the median moves from 115 to 114.5; the range grows from 8 to 28.

answers.text: Only the mode and the range
Only the median and the range
Only the mode and the median
The mode, median and range all change

questions.question_text: Which of these events does NOT have an equal chance of happening and not happening?
questions.explanation: P(two) = 1/6, while the other three events all have probability 1/2.

answers.text: Throwing a two with a fair six-sided die
Drawing a red card from a standard 52-card pack
Throwing an odd number with a fair die
Tossing a tail with a fair coin

questions.question_text: Q = {1, 2, 3, 4}. Which of these sets P satisfies P ∪ Q = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}?
questions.explanation: P must supply 5 and 6 and nothing outside {1,…,6}: P = {5, 6} works.
